section.title: The Day of the LORD and Elijah to Come
section.sectionRationale: The book's closing movement and the last words of the Old Testament. The Day of the LORD comes burning like a furnace against the arrogant, yet the sun of righteousness rises with healing for those who fear God's name. Then two final commands frame the four silent centuries to come: remember the law of Moses, and watch for Elijah, who will turn hearts before the great and awesome Day.
section.thesis: The coming Day of the LORD divides all people, fire for the arrogant and healing for those who fear God's name; and until it dawns God calls His people to remember His law and to await the Elijah who prepares the way, fulfilled in John the Baptist who heralds Christ.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the Day burning like a furnace against the arrogant; the sun of righteousness rising with healing for those who fear God; and the closing charge to remember Moses and to expect Elijah before the great Day.

units.label: The day burning like a furnace

verses.text: “For behold, the day is coming, burning like a {{tannur|furnace}}, when all the arrogant and every evildoer will be stubble; the day is coming when I will set them ablaze,” says the LORD of Hosts. “Not a root or branch will be left to them.”

units.label: The sun of righteousness
units.note: healing for those who fear His name

verses.text: “But for you who fear My name, the sun of {{tsedaqah|righteousness}} will rise with {{marpe|healing}} in its wings, and you will go out and leap like calves from the stall.
Then you will trample the wicked, for they will be ashes under the soles of your feet on the day I am preparing,” says the LORD of Hosts.

units.label: Remember Moses, watch for Elijah
units.note: the last words before the silence

verses.text: “Remember the law of My servant Moses, the statutes and ordinances I commanded him for all Israel at Horeb.
Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the coming of the great and awesome Day of the LORD.
And he will {{shuv|turn}} the hearts of the fathers to their children, and the hearts of the children to their fathers. Otherwise, I will come and strike the land with a {{cherem|curse}}.”

tannur.gloss: An oven or furnace. The Day of the LORD is pictured burning like a tannur, a fierce clay oven; the arrogant become stubble consumed within it, a vivid image of swift and decisive judgment on the proud.

tsedaqah.gloss: Righteousness, justice, right standing. The light that rises over those who fear God is the sun of tsedaqah, a righteousness that both judges evil and restores the faithful. Read as a title, it points to the Messiah in whom God's righteousness dawns.

marpe.gloss: Healing, a cure, restoration, health. The sun of righteousness rises with marpe, healing, in its wings. The same dawning light that consumes the proud brings wholeness and life to those who fear God's name.

shuv.gloss: To turn, return, restore. Here in the causative sense, to turn back: the promised Elijah will turn the hearts of fathers and children to one another, a work of repentance and restoration prepared before the great Day of the LORD.

cherem.gloss: A ban, something devoted to destruction, a curse. If hearts are not turned, God warns that He will strike the land with cherem, total devotion to judgment. This final Old Testament word is answered when Christ becomes a curse for us.

basic.context: Malachi closes with a vision of the Day of the LORD. For the arrogant and the evildoer it comes burning like a furnace, leaving neither root nor branch. But for those who fear God's name the sun of righteousness rises with healing in its wings, and they go out leaping like calves let out of the stall. Then come the last two commands of the Old Testament: remember the law of Moses, and watch for Elijah, whom God will send before the great and awesome Day to turn hearts back, lest the land be struck with a curse.
basic.hermeneutics: The Day of the LORD here has both a near and a final horizon: God's decisive intervention in judgment and salvation, ultimately in Christ's two comings. The sun of righteousness is best read as a person, the Messiah, who rises with healing. The promised Elijah is identified by Jesus as John the Baptist, so we read this closing prophecy through the Gospels it introduces.
basic.originalLanguages: The Day comes burning like a tannur, a furnace, against the arrogant. But for those who fear God the sun of tsedaqah, righteousness, rises with marpe, healing, in its wings. God promises to send Elijah to shuv, to turn, the hearts of fathers and children, lest He strike the land with a cherem, a curse of devotion to destruction.
basic.typology: The Day burning like a furnace points to the final Day of the LORD and judgment fulfilled at Christ's coming. The sun of righteousness rising with healing points to Christ, the light of the world and the great Physician. Elijah sent before the Day points to John the Baptist, who prepared the way for the Lord.
basic.christ: The sun of righteousness who rises with healing in His wings is Christ, in whom the dawn from on high has visited us. He is the light that judges the proud and heals those who fear God's name. The Elijah promised here came in John the Baptist, who turned hearts and pointed to Jesus. So the Old Testament ends looking straight at Christ, and after four silent centuries the Gospels open with His herald.
basic.god: God is just, coming in fiery judgment against arrogant evil. He is a healer and a Saviour to those who fear His name. He is patient, sending a messenger to turn hearts before the great Day. And He is faithful to His word, so that the Old Testament closes with a promise the Gospels take up.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 1:17 says John the Baptist would go before the Lord in the spirit and power of Elijah, turning the hearts of fathers to their children, directly citing verse 6. Matthew 11:13-14 and 17:10-13 identify John as the Elijah who was to come. Luke 1:78-79 speaks of the dawn from on high, the sunrise of God's mercy. Second Peter 3:7 speaks of the Day of judgment by fire.
basic.oneStory: The Old Testament ends the way the whole story is going: a Day of reckoning, a sunrise of healing for those who fear God, and a promised messenger. After four hundred silent years that messenger comes as John the Baptist, and the sun of righteousness rises in Jesus Christ, so the last page of the Old Testament and the first of the New reach for the same Saviour.

context.literary: The final movement of Malachi and the close of the Book of the Twelve and of the Old Testament. It answers the earlier complaint about God's justice with the certainty of the Day of the LORD, sets fire for the arrogant against healing for the God-fearing, and ends with a double command that points beyond the silence to come.
context.historical: Malachi speaks to a people who doubted that God would ever act. His closing vision assures them that the Day of the LORD is certain. These final words became the last God spoke through a prophet for some four hundred years, until John the Baptist broke the silence as the promised Elijah, preparing the way for Jesus.
context.canonical: The Day of the LORD theme runs through the prophets, from Joel and Amos to Zephaniah, and is taken up in the New Testament for the coming of Christ. The sending of Elijah is fulfilled in John the Baptist, Luke 1:17, Matthew 11:14, and 17:10-13, and the sun of righteousness is echoed in the dawn from on high in Luke 1:78.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure a doubting people that the Day of the LORD is coming, bringing judgment on the arrogant and healing to those who fear God, and to send them into the coming silence with two anchors: remember the law of Moses, and watch for the Elijah who will prepare the way before the great Day.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision of the Day describes what God will certainly do; the two closing commands, remember the law and await Elijah, are given as abiding instruction. The enduring truths are the certainty of judgment and salvation, the call to heed God's word, and the fulfillment of the Elijah promise in John the Baptist.

debates.question: Who is the Elijah God promises to send?

views.label: A literal return of Elijah
views.case: Some expect the historical prophet Elijah himself to reappear before the final Day, sometimes linked to the two witnesses of Revelation 11.

views.label: John the Baptist in the spirit and power of Elijah
views.case: Jesus plainly says John is the Elijah who was to come, Matthew 11:14 and 17:12-13, and Luke 1:17 describes John coming in Elijah's spirit and power; this is the primary fulfillment.

debates.bearing: The New Testament settles the main sense: John the Baptist fulfilled the promise as the herald of Christ, whatever further horizon some see before the last Day.

study.originalLanguages: The Day comes burning like a tannur, a furnace, and the arrogant become stubble, root and branch. But for those who fear God's name the sun of tsedaqah, righteousness, rises with marpe, healing, in its wings, and the freed people leap like calves. God then calls them to remember the law given at Horeb, and promises to send Elijah to shuv, to turn back, the hearts of fathers and children to one another, lest He come and strike the land with a cherem, a curse of utter devotion to destruction. The Old Testament closes on that word, curse, and waits four centuries for the One who would become a curse for us.

typology.type: The day burning like a furnace
typology.fulfillment: The Day of the LORD and final judgment, brought near in Christ, 2 Thessalonians 1:7-9 and 2 Peter 3:7.

typology.type: The sun of righteousness rising with healing
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the light of the world and dawn from on high, who heals those who fear God, Luke 1:78 and John 8:12.

typology.type: Elijah sent before the great Day
typology.fulfillment: John the Baptist, who came in the spirit and power of Elijah to prepare the way, Luke 1:17 and Matthew 11:14.

study.christ: The sun of righteousness who rises with healing in His wings is Jesus Christ, the dawn from on high who has visited us to give light to those in darkness. To the arrogant His coming is a consuming fire, but to those who fear God's name it is healing and freedom, like calves let loose from the stall. The Elijah God promised came in John the Baptist, who turned hearts and pointed to the Lamb of God. So the Old Testament ends gazing at Christ: after four hundred silent years the herald appears, and the Sun rises. The final word, curse, is answered at the cross, where Christ became a curse for us so that the blessing might come.
study.god: God is just, coming against arrogant evil like a furnace that leaves neither root nor branch. He is the healer and Saviour of all who fear His name, rising over them like the sun with healing in its wings. He is patient and gracious, sending a messenger to turn hardened hearts before the great and awesome Day. And He is utterly faithful to His word, closing the Old Testament with a promise that the Gospels open by fulfilling.

crossReferences.note: John the Baptist goes before the Lord in the spirit and power of Elijah, turning hearts, directly fulfilling Malachi 4:5-6.
Jesus says John the Baptist is the Elijah who was to come.
Jesus teaches that Elijah has already come in John the Baptist.
The dawn from on high gives light to those in darkness, echoing the sun of righteousness.

study.oneStory: The Old Testament ends the way the whole Bible moves: toward a Day of judgment and healing and a promised forerunner. After four hundred silent years John the Baptist comes as the Elijah, and the sun of righteousness rises in Jesus, who bears the curse so that those who fear God's name go out leaping in the light.

sermon.title: Malachi's Message On The Future (Malachi's Modern Message Part 8)
sermon.why: The final message of Legge's expository series through Malachi, covering 4:1-6, this closing passage directly. It expounds the Day of the LORD as burning for the arrogant and healing for the God-fearing, reads the sun of righteousness as Christ, and traces the Elijah promise through John the Baptist across the four hundred silent years into the Gospels.

quiz.q: What will the Day of the LORD be for the arrogant and evildoers?

options.text: A day burning like a furnace that leaves them neither root nor branch.
options.note: Verse 1 pictures decisive judgment on the proud.

options.text: A day of mild correction they will soon forget.
options.note: The image is complete consuming, not mild correction.

options.text: A day that never actually arrives.
options.note: The passage insists the Day is certainly coming.

options.text: A day of reward for their arrogance.
options.note: The opposite: it is judgment against the arrogant.

quiz.q: What will that day be for those who fear God's name?

options.text: The sun of righteousness will rise with healing in its wings, and they will go out leaping like calves.
options.note: Verse 2 promises healing and joyful freedom to the God-fearing.

options.text: A narrow escape with nothing to gain.
options.note: It brings healing and joy, far more than a bare escape.

options.text: The same fiery judgment as the wicked.
options.note: For them the same light means healing, not judgment.

options.text: Continued mourning and gloom.
options.note: The opposite: they leap like freed calves.

quiz.q: Who is the sun of righteousness?

options.text: Jesus Christ, the dawn from on high who rises with healing for those who fear God.
options.note: The New Testament links this rising light to Christ, Luke 1:78.

options.text: The nation of Israel restored to power.
options.note: The healing light is a person, the Messiah, not the nation itself.

options.text: A symbol of better weather for the crops.
options.note: It pictures the Messiah's coming, not merely improved harvests.

options.text: Any good ruler who brings peace.
options.note: It points specifically to Christ, not to rulers in general.

quiz.q: Who is the Elijah God promises to send before the great Day?

options.text: John the Baptist, who came in the spirit and power of Elijah to prepare the way for Christ.
options.note: Jesus identifies John as this Elijah, Matthew 11:14 and Luke 1:17.

options.text: The prophet Malachi himself.
options.note: The promised messenger is Elijah, fulfilled in John the Baptist, not Malachi.

options.text: A symbol with no real fulfillment.
options.note: The New Testament names a real fulfillment in John the Baptist.

options.text: The Messiah in person.
options.note: Elijah prepares the way before the Lord; he is the herald, not the Messiah.

quiz.q: What are the final commands that close the Old Testament?

options.text: Remember the law of Moses, and watch for the Elijah who will turn hearts before the great Day.
options.note: Verses 4 to 6 give both anchors before the silence that follows.

options.text: Rebuild the temple and resume the sacrifices.
options.note: The closing charge is to remember the law and await Elijah, not a building program.

options.text: Wait passively until the Day arrives.
options.note: The commands call for active remembering and watching, not passivity.

options.text: Forget the past and start a new religion.
options.note: The opposite: remember Moses and heed the coming messenger.

questions: The Day of the LORD is fire to the proud and healing to those who fear God. Which of those two do your own hopes and habits show you are living toward?
For those who fear His name the sun rises with healing. Where do you most need Christ's healing light to rise in your life right now?
God told His people to remember the law of Moses before the long silence. How does returning to God's word steady you when He seems quiet?
Elijah came to turn the hearts of fathers and children to one another. Whose heart, in your family, is God calling you to turn toward in reconciliation?
The Old Testament ends on the word curse, answered when Christ became a curse for us. How does the cross change the way you read the warning that closes Malachi?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnotes an alternate rendering, the Sun of Righteousness will rise with healing in His wings, reading the phrase as a title of the Messiah.
Horeb is another name for Mount Sinai, where the law was given; the BSB notes it may denote a mountain in that range.
Great and awesome is footnoted as also dreadful, with the Septuagint reading glorious; the Day of the LORD is both terrible and majestic.
The BSB notes this verse is cited in Luke 1:17 of John the Baptist, who came in the spirit and power of Elijah.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Malachi 4:1-6. All 6 verses reproduced in order. BSB footnotes at 4:2 (Sun of Righteousness as a title), 4:4 (Horeb as Mount Sinai), 4:5 (great and awesome, also dreadful, LXX glorious), and 4:6 (cited in Luke 1:17) are reflected in the translation notes. No em dashes occur in these verses.
